title: Listening - Part 3: Learning from Older People
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about something they learned from an older person. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what each speaker says they learned. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about something they learned from an older person. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H what each speaker says they learned. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about something an older person taught them.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: My grandmother never throws clothing away because of a missing button or small tear, while I used to replace things without thinking. When a favourite shirt tore, she asked me to bring it to her house. [19]She showed me how to choose matching thread, sew a neat repair and fix the button properly, and I realised that damaged clothing doesn't automatically need to be discarded.[/19] Since then I've repaired several things myself. My stitching isn't as tidy as hers, but my clothes last longer.||Speaker 2 Man: Our neighbour Mr Harris has grown vegetables in his garden for decades. Last year I tried planting tomatoes on my balcony, but they became weak and produced almost nothing. He noticed and offered advice. [20]He explained how much sunlight the plants needed, when to add food to the soil and why I had been watering them at the wrong time of day.[/20] This summer my plants are covered in tomatoes. I still ask him questions, and he seems pleased that somebody younger is interested.||Speaker 3 Woman: When I received my first regular pay from a weekend job, I immediately began planning clothes and concert tickets. My grandfather asked how much I intended to save, and I admitted I hadn't considered it. [21]He helped me create a simple budget, putting some money aside first and deciding how much I could spend each week without suddenly having nothing left.[/21] I still buy enjoyable things, but I now have savings for university, which makes me feel much more independent.||Speaker 4 Man: As a child I used to lose interest when my great-aunt began talking about relatives I had never met. Recently, for a school project, I interviewed her about her childhood during a period when our town changed greatly. [22]Listening properly made me realise that her memories weren't just old stories; they explained where my family came from and connected our lives to events I'd only read about in books.[/22] I recorded several conversations and now want to create a family history before those memories disappear.||Speaker 5 Woman: I had a summer job with a retired engineer who now runs a small repair workshop. I expected him mainly to teach me about tools. Instead, when I said I didn't know what career to choose, he talked about his own decisions. [23]He advised me to try different kinds of work before committing to a course, and to choose something that interested me rather than simply following the highest salary.[/23] As a result, I'm arranging more work experience before making my university applications.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: My grandmother never throws clothing away because of a missing button or small tear, while I used to replace things without thinking. When a favourite shirt tore, she asked me to bring it to her house. She showed me how to choose matching thread, sew a neat repair and fix the button properly, and I realised that damaged clothing doesn't automatically need to be discarded. Since then I've repaired several things myself. My stitching isn't as tidy as hers, but my clothes last longer.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: Our neighbour Mr Harris has grown vegetables in his garden for decades. Last year I tried planting tomatoes on my balcony, but they became weak and produced almost nothing. He noticed and offered advice. He explained how much sunlight the plants needed, when to add food to the soil and why I had been watering them at the wrong time of day. This summer my plants are covered in tomatoes. I still ask him questions, and he seems pleased that somebody younger is interested.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: When I received my first regular pay from a weekend job, I immediately began planning clothes and concert tickets. My grandfather asked how much I intended to save, and I admitted I hadn't considered it. He helped me create a simple budget, putting some money aside first and deciding how much I could spend each week without suddenly having nothing left. I still buy enjoyable things, but I now have savings for university, which makes me feel much more independent.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: As a child I used to lose interest when my great-aunt began talking about relatives I had never met. Recently, for a school project, I interviewed her about her childhood during a period when our town changed greatly. Listening properly made me realise that her memories weren't just old stories; they explained where my family came from and connected our lives to events I'd only read about in books. I recorded several conversations and now want to create a family history before those memories disappear.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I had a summer job with a retired engineer who now runs a small repair workshop. I expected him mainly to teach me about tools. Instead, when I said I didn't know what career to choose, he talked about his own decisions. He advised me to try different kinds of work before committing to a course, and to choose something that interested me rather than simply following the highest salary. As a result, I'm arranging more work experience before making my university applications.

options.text: how to be patient when learning
how to make a traditional dish
how to remain calm in an emergency
how to make decisions about work

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The grandmother taught the speaker basic sewing skills for mending clothing.

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: An experienced neighbour taught the speaker techniques for growing tomatoes successfully.

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The grandfather taught the speaker to budget and save part of her earnings.

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The great-aunt's memories helped the speaker understand the importance of family history.

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The older engineer provided guidance that influenced the speaker's approach to career decisions.
